data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-24T01:34:27.935Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me explain what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported — they were implemented as sync-over-async wrappers. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), there was an opt-in mechanism to allow this pattern. In .NET 11 (EF Core 11), this sync-over-async support is being removed entirely.
 
Wait, I need to reconsider the timeline here. Given that we're in April 2026, .NET 10 would have released in November 2025 and .NET 11 in November 2026. But actually, the breaking change pattern I'm thinking of started earlier — in EF Core 9, the Cosmos DB provider began throwing exceptions for synchronous operations by default, though you could opt back in to sync-over-async behavior. The progression likely continues from there through EF Core 10 and beyond.

The key point is that the sync-over-async opt-in is probably being removed entirely in the newer versions, which means any synchronous LINQ operations against Cosmos DB would throw exceptions with no fallback option except converting to async. That's the core breaking change the user needs to prepare for.
